speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I showed him my false papers, and they ask me all kind of questions, and my mother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's right, my mother didn't have false papers at that time, no. My mother didn't have false papers at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was talking in German-- it was very helpful-- she was talking to them saying that, I don't know, some story she gave them about her papers being in Warsaw, and she introduced me as her nephew through a marriage, OK? And persuaded them that, you know, that I wasn't Jewish, and they said to me-- the Gestapo's name was Fromer-- Herr Fromer-- that I can go to Warsaw and bring her papers. So they released me, and I, indeed, went to Warsaw to a friend of my mother who was doing those things, and he gave me false papers for her, with the name, Anka-- I have to look it up. I forgot her last name.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back to Krakow, went to the Gestapo headquarters, give him the papers, my mother's false papers, and he looked at them and said, fine, your mother will be released soon. I went to the apartment, my mother's apartment, and I had no money, no food, and I knew that in her wardrobe, she had some bottles of alcohol, and I could sell them. The closet had a Gestapo seal on it. I broke the seal, got the alcohol out, sold it, and had some money. The Volksdeutsche saw it, that I broke the seal. Anyhow, I was going to come back to it in a moment. Anyhow, I stayed in my mother's apartment, and of course, I wanted to communicate with her. So I went back to the prison, and talked to one of the Jewish policeman, and persuaded him to deliver a letter to my mother, which I scribbled down on a piece of paper, what I have done, that the papers are there, and so on, and he came back with a note from my mother. I forgot what it said. I put it in the pocket of my pants and went back to the apartment. Next morning, I was starting to dress myself. I just put my pants on, I didn't have my shirt on yet, when I see a car on the street coming, and-- and Herr Fromer is coming. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] On a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me go back for a moment, OK? Few-- few days had passed by, and my mother wasn't released. What do I do? I go back to the Gestapo headquarters, go to Mr. Fromer's office, and I said to him, where is my aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he puts his foot out, like this, so I had-- I had to cross it, and he said, you impertinent boy, or something like this. Get the hell out of here. How dare you come here and ask me questions like that. OK, so I went back to my mother's apartment. OK, that morning this car comes back, comes in, Mr. Fromer comes in. He looks immediately at the wardrobe, and says, you broke the seal. And I had this letter from my mother in my pocket, so  I-- so he says to me, empty your pockets.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I empty the pockets I had the letter, and I threw it back of me. The Jewish policeman saw it, and he picks it up, and gives it to Mr. Fromer. He asks the Jewish policeman to translate this letter to him. And he takes a horse whip he was holding with me and started beating me on my back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he told me to get dressed, and he took me back to the prison. My mother screamed when she saw me coming back in. Oh, no-- before-- before I got into the prison, he put all the Jewish policemen in one line, and he told me to identify the one that took the letter back to my mother. And he took out a gun and he said, if you don't tell me, I will kill you. And I didn't want to say who did it, so I looked at them and I said, look, there are several of them who looks very much alike. I don't know. And he just pushed me into the prison, at which moment Mother saw me and screamed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was punished for writing that letter by being locked up for the night in the rest room where there was water, and other things standing on the floor. It was a big, open kind of a thing. And she had to stand there for the whole night as a punishment. And I was put in one of the rooms with about, oh, 12 people. We had bunk beds that we slept in. The room was half the size of this room with a bucket where everybody was going to toilet, you know. Smelling-- the smell was unbelievable. And the mattresses were jumping with fleas, I mean, black with fleas all around jumping. The next morning, each one of us would take the mattress outside. There was this running-- it was about six feet between the fence and the building, and we would take some water and we would start drowning all the fleas in that bucket-- [LAUGHS] the bucket of water.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very hot, and my mother said to me-- she was released-- and she said to me, take off your shirt-- everybody is without a shirt. I didn't want her to see the welts on my back, and so I wouldn't take my shirt off. And she couldn't understand why I'm so modest. And she went to a friend of hers she met there. He said, talk to him-- she said to her, talk to Stephen. Why is he so modest suddenly? So I told her why I didn't want to take  my shirt off. And she somehow explained to my mother, don't worry about it, he'll be OK. So I never took my shirt off. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I was there for about two weeks. And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been the summer of '43. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] My mother had a very, very rich friend, Polish man, living in Krakow. And apparently he gave them thousands of zlotys and some gold, and we were released from the prison. One day, they just open the gate and says, you are free to go. Then later on we found out what has happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the journey you took back to Warsaw? I mean, it was during the war, what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the trains were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was it like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just going on a train, going there, going to a friend, saying what happened-- I need this thing. My mother gave me 500 zloty to pay for the document. Took the document, went back to Krakow. It was in two days. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, I have to tell you an incident while I was in the prison for two weeks. Mr. Fromer brought two children in, a girl about 12, maybe-- 11, 12-- a boy maybe 8-- 7, 8. And he started interrogating them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said-- he picked them up apparently on a streetcar stop. They were looking Jewish. And they said, where is your father? Where is your family? And the little boy said, my father was standing next to us at the streetcar stop when you took us. And he said, why didn't you say something? I wouldn't tell you he was my father. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I didn't see it, but I was told by people in the prison that before we came in, two Gestapo officers got drunk and they were playing football with a human head in the outside, out there, laughing. I didn't see that. But it certainly made everybody worry more and scared more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you weren't raised religiously, did you-- were you circumcised?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were never-- oh, so you never had worries of even that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There were time and again, I had to show it to the Gestapo. Had I been circumcised, I wouldn't have survived the war. My parents, I told you, were not religious, and they saw no reason for it. My mother saw no reason for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you don't mind--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: --perhaps you could tell an incidence when you were forced to prove--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there were  a number of them. Well, right there in prison I had to do that. And there was a time in which I gave my mother-- when I visited her in Krakow, said, let's do something, a movie. So we went to a German theatre, a German movie house, and I saw a movie called I won't ever forget it. It was a story of the man who invented diesel engines. And even though I didn't understand a word of what he said-- it was in German-- I was very impressed and I understood what was going on. As we were leaving the theatre, we were approached by two Germans, and I don't know how they figured out that we were non-German. But again, they took us to the side, and I had to show my penis, and my mother had to give them a golden bracelet. And they let us go. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were we? It was after the two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, we were released there, and OK, so I went to-- I went back to Kielce to the factory, and my mother stayed in Krakow. Well, this was the time, I think, that my mother should have left Krakow. She had an opportunity to cross the border and go into Russia. She was given that opportunity, and she refused because she didn't want to abandon me. And that was a big mistake. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Again, my mother found some Polish baron-- or not a baron, no, it's like a-- not quite a prince, but a count, a count, Polish count. And amazingly enough, the Germans didn't confiscated his belongings. And he-- so she realized that I was undernourished and she somehow got permission from them to go visit them for one week. And they fed me and they fed me-- for one week, meals five times a day. And you know, I was hungry. And then I realized what was the matter-- because when I was in Kielce, I was eating this huge pot of soup in the-- at noontime, and a huge one in the evening. And my stomach was full. When I ate, instead eggs and cheeses and meats, there was nothing fooling my stomach, and I felt hungry. [LAUGHS] But after one week, marvelous week, I went back to Kielce, and-- and was there. My mother writes me a letter saying she found an agricultural school. It was the only school still allowed in Poland during that time. And she knew that in that agricultural school, even though they were teaching you the agriculture, they still teached you subjects like physics and mathematics and others. Somehow, again, she found the money to pay for my schooling and she told me to go there. She told me where it was, she enrolled me there, and I should go there. Trzyciarz, what I hate even to spell it for you, to you, T-R-Z-Y-C-I-A-R-Z-- a small village. I went there, and it was lucky I did, because after the war I went back to those people in Kielce I lived with, they told me that one week after I left, Gestapo came looking for me. They already knew that my papers were false, because they already entered the Russian zone and they already-- and the Gestapo was amazing, they had-- they didn't have computers at those times, but they were absolutely amazing in following through on those things. And they went there, they found out no such person wasn't ever there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in the school and this was a marvelous time for me. Plenty of food to eat, work, but I was learning things. I was A student in physics, and chemistry, and algebra, and so on. There were about, oh, a dozen students there. We lived in a dormitory. I shared a room with three other-- two other kids. Made good friends with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was really very marvelous [SIGHS] until-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] [DRINKS] July 11 [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] in 1944, 3 and the wife of the director called me in and gave me a letter from my mother. [CRYING] My mother said she was arrested. She was in prison. They found out that the person she had papers for was dead, so she told them her real name. She told me not to try to look her up or see her, because too dangerous. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [CRYING] She told me that I should remember what she always taught me, that the world belongs to the brave. I should be brave. [DRINKS] [CRYING] It will be difficult for me, but [SIGHS] she wrote to her friend with whom she was working in an office in Krakow, and she told her what to do-- namely-- again, she found somebody in the prison who, for money, would smuggle a letter. And she wrote a letter to that friend to what to do. And last night I translated it. This is the letter that-- this is a Xerox of that letter which she wrote. I don't know where she found this paper in the prison. I don't know where she found the pen. It's amazing. But again, she spoke German, and as you see from the letter, she immediately was offered a job in the prison by the Germans as a translator. The name of the friend of hers was Krisia. She said, 'Dear Krisia, I am on Montelupich.' Montelupich was a German prison in Krakow. 'I will be going to Plaszow'-- Plaszow was the other prison where we were for two weeks before-- 'or will be staying here and will be working in the office. Because they have found that Anka is not living, I have told them my real name. They know the address of my relative, Stephen. Krisia, go to him this Saturday. Perhaps you will still make it in time. He should go find work on the estate of the count, or in your place, anywhere, even the Baudinst.' Baudinst was a German work place that they got people to work on German programs. And with my false papers, I guess, and without them looking any deeper into it, they probably would have kept me there and I would work there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'Perhaps he will succeed to cover his steps and then go to Chernigov.' That's another city there. 'I do not need anything, only peace of mind [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that he's free. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I will not burden you with anything else, only this one time. If you will not be able to go Saturday, you must go on a weekday. Give Stephen my postcard to read. He will be brave and won't be preoccupied with me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Give 200 zlotys to the man who will give you this letter. Keep in touch with him. I want to know what will happen to Stephen. Go to our office quickly, take our photo-- no, take out photo from my desk and give it to Stephen. There are two pieces-- there are three pieces of soap in the desk drawer. Sell them for 120 zloty. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] You will have enough for this man for two times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ask him if he has sent Stephen 120 zloty I gave him. If not, ask for it firmly and give them to Stephen. I also have sent Stephen 400 zloty. Did he receive it? He should not pay the school director, but keep the money to himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I have another name now. I'm informing you and Mr. Wladyslaw B.'-- I don't know who he is-- 'he, if he wants to, may send a parcel from time to time to Romana Halperin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Warm gloves for Stephen can be picked up on the [NON-ENGLISH] Square, third floor, Maria Sufa. A sweater and handkerchiefs [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] are [NON-ENGLISH]. They will try to get a confession from Stephen by beating him that I'm his mother, but he cannot admit to something that is not true. Krisia, I know that you have it not easy yourself, but have pity on him, bless him on his new part of life.' [CRYING] That was the last I heard. [DRINKS] [SIGHS] I was an A student, and this was three weeks before the final exams. And I failed them all. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the director told the teachers that I have lost my mother. And they gave me completion, that they knew because they felt that I was an A student all  the time, and they understood why I couldn't do the final exams. The director and his wife were marvelous. They obviously realized what has happened and they told me I can stay there as long as I want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was working on the farm. I didn't go anywhere. I don't know why the Germans didn't come after me. I don't understand what Mother meant, they know my address. I don't know. They  didn't come.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe because it was too late in the war, they already were trying to escape, which the Russian front was already coming. It's possible that that's the reason why they decided they couldn't bother going to this little village and finding me there, but I stayed there. And the end of the war came in January 18, 1945, when the Russian front came by. Before that the Germans would take us and have us to build-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know what the name of it is. They were supposed to have a line of defense so they made us build-- dig big crisscross kind of a things in which soldiers would be there and defending and so on. They got the farmers to cut trees in the forest and build fortifications, and I was given little cards. And I was standing there, and I was supposed to give each of the farmers a card each time they brought a tree. And they had to bring a minimum of three trees a day before they were able to go home. And I, of course, would give them two cards, hoping that the Germans are not going to find out-- which they didn't-- so they could go home sooner. There was a part of Poland which was annexed to Germany, and there was a part of Poland which was-- I don't know what they call it. In Polish it was [POLISH], which is part of Poland which was under the German occupation but was not part of Germany. And the border was about 20 kilometers from where we were. So in order for me to, again, make some money, I would take a wagon with a horse, and I would go across the border-- we were able to do that-- and smuggle food back and forth. And I remember one incident in which I bought a goose in the German part, and put it on my wagon, and covered it with a thing, and I was about to leave when the Germans passed by and the goose started [LAUGHS] making noise. So of course, they discovered me, but all I had to do is to give them the goose, and they left, and I came back to the school. It wasn't the worst of times. It was scary time. The German-- the Russian front came and the artillery barrage started. And we were sitting in the kitchen of the director's house, and I was making butter-- you know, the thing that you pull up and down, up and down. I was doing that. And one of the shells-- oh, by the way, we have learned very quickly which were the German shells and which were the Russian shells, because when the Russians were shooting, there was this explosion, the noise, and then you could hear this wind kind of swish sound. When the Germans were shooting, you could hear the swish sound first and then was the explosion. [LAUGHS] So we learned very quickly which one is dangerous. But anyhow, one of the shells exploded right outside the kitchen with a horrendous thing, and I was doing this butter. Everybody in the kitchen went panicky, went under the table. And I was sitting there making the butter, and I wouldn't stop, because I had to make the butter. And this became so proverbial that every time something happened in the future, they were referring to me making this butter. I mean, this follow me even here, because some friends learned about it. But my philosophy was, once I heard the explosion and I was still alive, there was nothing to do. [LAUGHS] If it hit me, I wouldn't even know about it. It was my philosophy at that time. OK. So the war ended January 18, 1945, and that's the end of my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now  we're going to change reels.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
